text: The surgeon applies a clip to the proximal section point, which is identified through a suprapubic mini laparotomy incision.

The surgeon releases the trisangle, revealing the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) within the surgical field.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection maneuvers to separate the embryological plane between the Toldt fascia and Denonvilliers fascia, located below the inferior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) as the medial limit of the dissection, marking the boundary for further surgical maneuvers.

The surgeon identifies the first sigmoid artery, then uses a coagulation device to cauterize it before sectioning the artery.

The surgeon begins intramesocolic dissection just below the colonic wall, maintaining close contact with the bowel above the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).

The surgeon individually coagulates and sections the sigmoid arterial branches, ensuring precise control over each vessel during the procedure.

The surgical technique is demonstrated to preserve the nerves surrounding the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and those ascending from the pelvic plexus, ensuring careful dissection around these structures.
The surgeon encounters challenges in the dissection due to thickened mesocolon and hypervascularization, likely resulting from previous abscesses and inflammation.

The surgeon reaches the upper rectum, surrounding it from above, and includes the rectum-sigmoid junction in the resection area.

The surgeon maintains the section plane above the upper rectal artery, approaching the proximal rectum from above while leaving the mesorectum intact.

The surgeon prepares the colon for distal sectioning below the rectum sigmoid junction, an area characterized by greater intraluminal pressure near the sphincter.

The surgeon uses a mechanical stapler to transect the bowel, with multiple refills of the stapler sometimes required to complete the cut.

The surgeon externalizes the colon through a suprapubic incision, with a clip marking the proximal section margin for identification.

The surgeon performs a straight mechanical colorectal anastomosis, connecting the proximal colon to the rectum using a stapling device.

The surgeon adjusts the orientation of the colon axis to ensure proper alignment for the next surgical step.

The surgeon closes the mesocolic bridge using surgical glue and fires the stapler to complete the anastomosis. The integrity of the anastomosis is tested by transanal irrigation with methylene blue or the Ehrlich test, observing for any leaks.

The surgeon tests the colorectal anastomosis by performing transanal irrigation using methionine blue, visually assessing the integrity of the anastomosis for any leaks.
The surgeon performs a perioperative rectoscopy to visually inspect the anastomosis site, ensuring there is no bleeding from the colorectal anastomosis.
